text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, with the gallbladder visible at the base of the liver. Dissection is underway in Calot's triangle to separate the cystic duct and cystic artery.
In a four-port laparoscopic cholecystectomy, the surgeon successfully separates the cystic duct and applies clips to secure it.
The surgeon applies three clips to the cystic duct, with two clips remaining inside the body and one attached to the gallbladder for removal. Space is created for clipping the cystic artery next.
The surgeon places the first clip on the cystic artery, with a visible lymph node nearby. The white structure seen above the lymph node is clearly identifiable as the lymph node itself.
The surgeon cuts the cystic duct and proceeds to dissect the gallbladder from the cystic plate, preparing to remove it.
The surgeon observes a slight black leakage and continues dissection using a Hickory instrument.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder using ovum forceps and extracts stones from it. The surgical field is irrigated, and a drain is placed. The pneumoperitoneum is deflated, and the alpha port incisions are closed.
